title: Chemex Coffee: Why The Filter Costs $0.50 ☕📈
description:
The Chemex bonded paper filters are 20-30% thicker than competitors. The science of why this matters for flavor, oil filtration, and the cleanest pour-over cup possible.

Sources: Chemex Corporation, James Hoffmann, SCA brewing research, Peter Schlumbohm history.

segments.voice: The Chemex paper filter costs fifty cents per brew. The V60 filter costs ten cents. The price difference comes down to filter thickness. Chemex filters are twenty to thirty percent thicker than standard filters. That thickness changes the coffee in measurable ways that other filters cannot replicate.

Peter Schlumbohm invented the Chemex in 1941. The German-American chemist designed the brewer based on laboratory glass. The hourglass shape with a wooden collar became an icon of mid-century design. The Chemex sits in the Museum of Modern Art permanent collection in New York City.

The Chemex paper filter is significantly thicker than V60 or Melitta filters. The bonded paper construction prevents oils, fines, and sediment from passing through. The cup ends up as the cleanest possible pour-over, with no body from suspended particles or oils.

The thickness creates slower drainage compared to V60. Total Chemex brew time runs four to five minutes, longer than V60's three minute target. The longer brew allows full extraction even with the larger water volume that Chemex's bigger capacity accommodates.

Brewing in Chemex starts with the filter setup. The folded paper filter has three layers on one side and one layer on the other. Always position the three-layer side against the spout. The thicker side reinforces the area where weight concentrates during brewing.

Pre-rinse the Chemex filter thoroughly with hot water before brewing. The thick paper holds significant paper flavor when dry. A single rinse removes most paper taste. Some brewers double rinse for delicate light roast coffees where any paper character would distract from origin notes.
segments.visual: Chemex filter pre rinse hot water

segments.voice: James Hoffmann recommends a Chemex ratio of one to fifteen for filter coffee. Forty grams of coffee with six hundred grams of water for a typical six cup brew. Water temperature ninety-three Celsius. Medium-coarse grind, slightly coarser than V60. Total brew time around five minutes.

The bloom phase in Chemex uses twice the coffee weight in water. Eighty grams of water for forty grams of coffee. Pour in slow spirals to wet all grounds. Wait forty-five seconds. The CO2 release from fresh coffee bubbles visibly through the bloom.

Main pours in Chemex follow the bloom in three stages. Pour to two hundred grams by one minute thirty. Pour to four hundred grams by two minutes thirty. Pour to six hundred grams by three minutes thirty. The full brew finishes draining between four minutes thirty and five minutes.

Grind size for Chemex sits slightly coarser than V60. The thicker filter would clog with too-fine grounds, extending brew time excessively. Medium-coarse, similar to sea salt, works best. If brew takes longer than five minutes thirty seconds, coarsen the grind. If faster than four minutes, fine the grind.

The cup profile of Chemex coffee is unmistakably clean. The oils filtered out by the thick paper leave a tea-like clarity. Bright acidity and aromatics stand out without competing body. Some drinkers find the cup too thin. Others love the absolute clarity of flavor.

The Chemex glass body comes in three to thirteen cup sizes. Three cup, six cup, eight cup, ten cup, and thirteen cup designate water capacity. The six and eight cup sizes are most popular for home use. Larger sizes suit office settings or family service.

The wooden collar and leather tie are removable for cleaning. Some Chemex models use glass handles instead of wood. Both versions function identically. The wooden collar prevents burning your hand on the hot glass. The leather tie holds the wood firmly to the neck.

Cleaning a Chemex requires care due to the all-glass construction. Hand wash with warm soapy water. Avoid sudden temperature changes that could crack the glass. Some users put Chemex in the dishwasher but the manufacturer recommends hand washing. The brewer lasts decades with proper care.

The all-glass construction means Chemex serves as both brewer and server. The brewed coffee can sit in the bottom chamber for serving without flavor transfer. Glass doesn't impart flavor like some metal or plastic alternatives. The temperature drops gradually, allowing the cup to evolve as it cools.

The Chemex method excels with delicate light roast coffees. Ethiopian Yirgacheffe, Kenyan AA, and Colombian Pink Bourbon shine in Chemex. The clean cup reveals subtle notes that pour-over and French press would mask. Dark roast coffees often taste thin in Chemex, lacking expected body.

Water quality matters enormously for Chemex. The clean cup highlights any off-flavors in the water. SCA recommended water with one hundred fifty parts per million TDS and balanced minerals works best. Chlorinated tap water produces a clearly worse cup than bottled or filtered alternatives.

The Chemex Ottomatic is an electric Chemex brewing system. The machine handles water heating, pour scheduling, and bloom timing automatically. The brewer costs around four hundred dollars. Results are consistent but slightly less customizable than manual brewing. Most enthusiasts prefer manual control.

Chemex filter alternatives exist but rarely match the original. Third-party paper filters often lack the thickness and bonding that defines the Chemex cup. Reusable metal filters change the cup character significantly, allowing oils through and producing a French press style brew.

The squared Chemex paper filter and circular filter both exist. Squared filters fit older Chemex models. Circular filters fit modern models. Both produce similar results. Some users find squared filters easier to position correctly with the three-layer side facing the spout.

Chemex coffee storage in the brewer maintains temperature for thirty to sixty minutes. After that, the coffee cools below ideal serving temperature. The clean cup means the flavor degrades slowly compared to French press or moka pot. Serve fresh for best results.

The Chemex versus V60 debate continues among brewing enthusiasts. V60 produces a slightly fuller cup due to thinner filters and the spiral ridges. Chemex produces a cleaner cup with the thick filter. Both methods work well. Pick based on cup style preference.

Common Chemex mistakes include not pre-rinsing the filter, pouring on the paper instead of the coffee bed, using too-fine grind that causes clogging, using stale coffee, and removing the filter before all coffee drains through. Each mistake produces a measurably worse cup.

The cost of Chemex ownership runs about fifty cents per brew in filters. For one brew per day, that totals one hundred eighty dollars per year just in filters. Some users switch to reusable metal filters to reduce ongoing cost, accepting the change in cup character.

But there is something Chemex devotees rarely admit. The thick filter that creates the legendary clean cup also removes flavor compounds that some drinkers want in their coffee. The Chemex cup is technically pure but flavor-thinner than other methods. The trade is real and not universally preferred.

To master Chemex, use fresh light roast coffee, pre-rinse generously, weigh everything, time everything, and brew slowly. The cup will be the cleanest pour-over you have ever tasted. Next video, the French press fines reducing technique that Hoffmann perfected. Subscribe.
